publicationstmt.publisher: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

profiledesc.creation: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on 2023-10-18 00:16:56 UTC.

repository.corpname: Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections

did.abstract: The Chicago street gang collection contains photographs, newspaper clippings, memorial and business cards, ephemera, fabric patches, clothes, and inscribed books, documenting activities of the Chicago Gaylords (1953- ), as well as other street gangs in the Chicago area, dating from the late 1970s to the early 1980s.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Archival materials were preserved by a street gang member active in the North Side of Chicago in the 1970s and early 1980s, likely a member of the Gaylords (1953- ), one of Chicago's oldest and largest street gangs, founded by World War II veterans in the 1950s. Most of the original members were of Italian, Irish, or Greek ancestry. The Gaylords Street gang had its beginnings on Chicago's North Side, gradually expanding its territory into the South Side. In the late 1970s to early 1980s, the group was located north and south with several sections, such as Lawndale and Altgeld streets (Logan Square), Moffat & Campbell streets (Humboldt Park), and more. Colors featured in the clothing and patches provide information about a member's affiliation and, in some cases, their activities. Gaylords symbols, such as the Celtic Cross, can be found as tags in murals, clothing patches, and business cards. Slogans included "Cross is the boss." Numbers 7 and 12 are used because, per the name "GayLords", the letters G and L are the 7th and 12th letters of the alphabet. Each set/section had its leader (or leaders). It was divided mainly into five groups: seniors, juniors, peewees, midget, and SlyLords/Puds.

scopecontent.head: Scope and content
scopecontent.p: The Chicago street gang collection documents the activities of the Chicago Gaylords (or Almighty Gaylords) (1953- ), as well as other Chicago street groups, such as the Noble Knights and the Simon City Royals, dating from the late 1970s to the early 1980s, when the Gaylords were most active. The collection contains two photograph albums, both including tags and artwork on the inside covers, an album of newspaper clippings about gang-related news, loose color photographs, and newspaper clippings, tag and mural artwork, memorial and business cards of members of over 30 gang groups, ephemera, fabric patches, a t-shirt and a letterman-style sweater featuring war colors. There are also two publications by former Gaylords member Michael Scott, inscribed with signatures, tags, and messages by fellow members.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Chicago street gang collection, #8133. Division of Rare and Manuscript Collections, Cornell University Library.

did.unittitle: Chicago gangs' activity related newspaper articles

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes newspaper printed, group photos of various gangs, such as the Latin Eagles, Gaylords, Latin Panther and Holstein Park, The Spanish Cobras, and more.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes mural-tag artwork on both inside covers.

did.unittitle: Gaylords-Lawndale & Altgeld section, Album of Newspaper Clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes mural-tag artwork on both inside covers.

did.unittitle: Noble Knights- Cicero Section, Album of Photos and Newspaper Clippings

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes photos from Noble Knights Club House, collector's home, on the street, murals, and cars.

did.unittitle: Photograph of Latin King gang in Humbolt Park Area, Chicago.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Playground at Lowell school near beach and Spaulding. Chicago Sun-Times.

did.unittitle: Photographs of gang members, graffiti murals, and street corners

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 50 color photos of gang members (10 of them, polaroid photos); 38 color photos of gang graffiti murals and street corners.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Some of the cards include tags written on the back. Cards from 28 gangs in single cards and groups of the same gang: Single Cards (10 pages, 74 cards); Group 1: Ambrose (3 cards, 1 page); Group 2: C-notes (28 cards, 4 pages); Group 3: Deuces (9 cards, 2 pages); Group 4: Gaylords (85 cards, 11 pages); Group 5: Gaylords, Palmer St. (5 cards, 1 page); Group 6: Harrison Gents (4 cards, 1 page); Group 7: Hell Devils (3 cards, 1 page); Group 8: Imperial Gangsters (2 cards, 1 page); Group 9: Insane (16 cards, 2 pages); Group 10: Jokers (3 cards, 1 page); Group 11: Jousters, Three Almighty, Taylor St. (12 cards,2 pages); Group 12: Latin Brothers (5 cards, 1 page); Group 13: Latin Kings (2 cards, 1 page); Group 14: Noble Knights (22 cards, 3 pages); Group 15: Party Boys (4 cards. 1 page); Group 16: Party People (3 cards, 1 page); Group 17: Playboys (14 cards, 2 pages); Group 18: Police-related cards (4 cards, 1 page); Group 19: Popes (7 cards. 1 page); Group 20: Racine Boys (2 cards, 1 page); Group 21: Royal Capris (10 cards, 2 pages); Group 22: Simon City Royals (30 cards, 4 pages); Group 23-p.1: Stone Greaser (5 cards, 1 page); Group 24: Stoned Freaks (2 cards, 1 page); Group 25: Throop Boy's (3 cards, 1 page); Group 26: Two Six Boys (3 cards, 1 page); Group 27: Villa Lobos (5 cards, 1 page); Group 28-p.1: 12th St Players (4).

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: The cards were divided by the donor into single and gang-group cards. This initial arrangement is maintained. Group numbers are indicated at the top left of each page, and each group starts at a new page. Researchers can place an archival blank letter-size page, included in the box, at the back of each plastic storage page for better visibility.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Letterman-style sweater embellished with Insane and Gaylords gang symbols in light blue and black; Cotton T-Shirt with a Simon City Royals stamp in black color with blue letters.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Simon City Royals black strip with light blue letters; Patches of crosses; Roman Numerical Patches (XII and VII) in light blue color; Patches of the following gang groups: Lords, Almighty, Masters, and Latin Kings; Spanish and Lords; Lords North and Masters Westside; Almighty Hustlers N' Gangsters; Harrison Gents; Tiny Latin Counts.

did.unittitle: Mural Artwork, Chicago Star Police magazine, and other documents

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Includes a Chicago Street Gang typed list of 79 gang groups. The magazine has a street gang puzzle on the back page. Mural Artworks are mainly pen/pencil drawings. A photo by Jim Frost (Chicago Sun-Times), taken in the Humboldt Park area, shows Latin Kings' Mural tag. This photo is oversized.

did.unittitle: Scott, Michael. Lords of Lawndale: My Life in a Chicago White Street Gang. Bloomington, Ind.: Author House, 2008.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Book inscribed with signatures, tags and messages by gang members.

did.unittitle: Scott, Michael. Lords of Of Kilbourn. Wasteland Press, 2008.
